questions.stem: A 32-year-old man with HIV (CD4 count 45 cells/µL) presents with a 2-week history of frontal headache, fever, and photophobia. He is not on antiretroviral therapy. On examination, he has neck stiffness and Kernig's sign is positive. CT head is unremarkable. Lumbar puncture shows an opening pressure of 28 cmH₂O, CSF white cell count 20/µL (lymphocytes predominant), CSF protein 0.7 g/L, CSF glucose 2.0 mmol/L (serum glucose 5.0 mmol/L). India ink stain is positive. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The combination of HIV with severe immunosuppression, raised CSF opening pressure, lymphocytic pleocytosis, low CSF glucose, and positive India ink is classic for cryptococcal meningitis. Tuberculous meningitis typically has a more subacute course, higher CSF protein, and may have evidence of tuberculosis elsewhere.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old immunocompetent woman presents with a 3-week history of progressively worsening headache, intermittent nausea, and blurred vision. She is afebrile and has no neck stiffness. Fundoscopy reveals papilledema. CT head shows no mass lesion. Lumbar puncture: opening pressure 30 cmH₂O, CSF clear, white cells 10/µL (lymphocytes), protein 0.45 g/L, glucose 3.2 mmol/L (serum glucose 5.5 mmol/L). Cryptococcal antigen test is positive. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: This immunocompetent patient with subacute headache, raised intracranial pressure, papilledema, and positive cryptococcal antigen is typical for cryptococcal meningitis. The absence of fever and subtle CSF findings can be misleading; the diagnosis is confirmed by antigen testing. Multiple sclerosis and sarcoidosis do not explain the positive antigen.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man with HIV (CD4 count 30 cells/µL) not on ART presents with a 1-week history of severe headache, vomiting, and confusion. On examination, GCS 12, bilateral papilledema, and a left sixth nerve palsy. CT head shows no mass, but ventricles are small. Lumbar puncture: opening pressure >40 cmH₂O, CSF white cells 5/µL, protein 0.8 g/L, glucose 1.8 mmol/L. Cryptococcal antigen is positive. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Cryptococcal meningitis with raised intracranial pressure
questions.explanation: The markedly elevated opening pressure, cranial nerve palsy, and papilledema in the context of HIV and positive cryptococcal antigen indicate cryptococcal meningitis complicated by severe raised ICP. Toxoplasmosis and lymphoma typically cause focal lesions on imaging, which is absent here.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old renal transplant recipient on tacrolimus and prednisolone presents with a 5-day history of headache, fever, and confusion. He has no focal neurological signs. CT head is unremarkable. Lumbar puncture: opening pressure 25 cmH₂O, CSF white cells 15/µL (lymphocytes), protein 0.6 g/L, glucose 2.5 mmol/L. CSF Gram stain shows encapsulated yeasts. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The patient is immunocompromised due to transplant and immunosuppression. Encapsulated yeasts on Gram stain are highly suggestive of Cryptococcus. CMV encephalitis typically presents with altered mental status but CSF findings are often nonspecific; PML is caused by JC virus with no CSF pleocytosis; aspergillosis usually presents with focal lesions or hemorrhage.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man with known HIV (CD4 count 20 cells/µL) was diagnosed with cryptococcal meningitis 2 weeks ago and commenced on amphotericin B and flucytosine. He was started on antiretroviral therapy 1 week ago. Now he presents with worsening headache, fever, and confusion. Repeat lumbar puncture: opening pressure 35 cmH₂O, CSF white cells 50/µL (lymphocytes), protein 0.9 g/L, glucose 2.0 mmol/L. CSF culture is negative. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The timing after starting ART and the finding of negative CSF culture despite clinical worsening point to IRIS, not relapse. IRIS is an inflammatory response to residual cryptococcal antigen. Drug-induced meningitis is rare with these agents. Toxoplasmosis and lymphoma would not explain the negative culture and timing.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old man with HIV (CD4 count 50 cells/µL) is asymptomatic. He is not on antiretroviral therapy. According to UK guidelines, which single investigation is most appropriate for the early detection of cryptococcal meningitis in this patient?
questions.explanation: In HIV patients with CD4 <100, serum cryptococcal antigen (CrAg) screening is recommended as it is non-invasive and detects antigenemia before symptoms develop. Lumbar puncture is indicated only if symptomatic or if antigen positive.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man with known HIV (not on therapy) presents to the emergency department with a 3-day history of headache, fever, and confusion. On examination, he has neck stiffness and photophobia. What is the single most important investigation to confirm the diagnosis of cryptococcal meningitis?
options: Full blood count and C-reactive protein
Lumbar puncture for CSF examination (including India ink, cryptococcal antigen, and culture)
questions.explanation: In a patient with signs of meningitis and HIV, CSF analysis is the definitive test to confirm cryptococcal meningitis. Although CT may be done first if focal signs, the question asks for the test to confirm diagnosis. Serum CrAg is positive but does not confirm meningitis.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman who is a renal transplant recipient on tacrolimus and prednisolone presents with a 2-week history of progressive headache and low-grade fever. She has no neck stiffness. Neurological examination is normal. Which investigation is most likely to confirm the diagnosis of cryptococcal meningitis?
options: Lumbar puncture for CSF cryptococcal antigen
questions.explanation: In immunocompromised patients with subacute symptoms, CSF cryptococcal antigen has high sensitivity and specificity for meningitis. Serum CrAg may be positive but CSF is confirmatory. Distractor: serum CrAg is tempting but less specific for CNS involvement.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old man with HIV and suspected cryptococcal meningitis undergoes lumbar puncture. The CSF India ink stain is negative, but the CSF cryptococcal antigen is positive. What is the most likely explanation for this discrepancy?
options: The patient has a low fungal burden
The patient has a different fungal infection
The positive antigen is a false positive
The India ink stain was performed incorrectly
questions.explanation: India ink stain has a lower sensitivity (approximately 50-80%) compared to cryptococcal antigen, so a negative India ink with positive antigen suggests a low number of organisms (low fungal burden). False positives are rare due to high specificity.

questions.stem: In a patient with HIV and confirmed cryptococcal meningitis, CSF opening pressure is measured during lumbar puncture. At what opening pressure threshold is there an increased risk of neurological complications and is considered elevated?
questions.explanation: In cryptococcal meningitis, opening pressure >20 cmH2O is considered elevated and is associated with increased morbidity. While some guidelines use >25 cmH2O for intervention, >20 cmH2O is the diagnostic threshold for elevated ICP.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old immunocompetent man presents with a 2-week history of headache, fever, and neck stiffness. CSF analysis shows elevated opening pressure, lymphocytic pleocytosis, low glucose, and Indian ink stain is positive for Cryptococcus neoformans. His renal function is normal. What is the most appropriate first-line induction therapy?
options: Liposomal amphotericin B 3 mg/kg/day IV + flucytosine 25 mg/kg orally four times daily
Amphotericin B deoxycholate 0.7 mg/kg/day IV + fluconazole 800 mg daily
questions.explanation: Liposomal amphotericin B plus flucytosine is the recommended first-line induction regimen in the UK for non-pregnant, non-HIV patients with normal renal function (BSH guidelines). Amphotericin B deoxycholate is an alternative but not first-line; fluconazole monotherapy is inferior and reserved for resource-limited settings.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old pregnant woman (28 weeks gestation) presents with cryptococcal meningitis confirmed on CSF culture. She is HIV-negative and has normal renal function. Which agent is contraindicated in her induction regimen?
questions.explanation: Flucytosine is teratogenic in animal studies and contraindicated in pregnancy (BNF). Liposomal amphotericin B is safe and first-line. Fluconazole high dose may be associated with fetal harm but is not absolutely contraindicated; flucytosine is clearly contraindicated.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man with HIV (CD4 count 50 cells/µL) was diagnosed with cryptococcal meningitis 3 weeks ago and started on liposomal amphotericin B and flucytosine. Antiretroviral therapy was initiated 2 weeks after diagnosis. He now presents with recurrent headache, fever, and neck stiffness. CSF opening pressure is normal, and CSF culture is negative for Cryptococcus. What is the most appropriate therapy?
options: Increase the dose of liposomal amphotericin B
questions.explanation: The presentation is consistent with immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS) in an HIV patient on ART after recent cryptococcal meningitis. Corticosteroids are recommended for management of paradoxical IRIS. Lumbar puncture shows no raised pressure and culture negative, ruling out relapse.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old HIV-positive man has completed 8 weeks of induction and consolidation therapy for cryptococcal meningitis (liposomal amphotericin B + flucytosine for 2 weeks, then fluconazole 400 mg daily for 6 weeks). His CD4 count is currently 120 cells/µL. What is the most appropriate maintenance therapy to prevent relapse?
questions.explanation: Secondary prophylaxis with fluconazole 200 mg daily is recommended for HIV patients until CD4 count >200 cells/µL for at least 3 months. Fluconazole is the drug of choice for maintenance.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old HIV-positive man with cryptococcal meningitis develops acute kidney injury (AKI) with creatinine 450 µmol/L during induction therapy with liposomal amphotericin B and flucytosine. His weight is 70 kg. What is the most appropriate alternative induction regimen?
questions.explanation: In patients who develop amphotericin B-related nephrotoxicity, the preferred alternative is fluconazole (high dose, 1200 mg daily) combined with flucytosine for 2 weeks. Amphotericin B deoxycholate is more nephrotoxic. Fluconazole monotherapy is less effective.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man with known HIV (CD4 30, not on ART) presents with a 5-day history of headache, fever, and mild confusion. Glasgow Coma Scale 14. No focal neurology. CT head normal. Lumbar puncture shows opening pressure 30 cm H₂O, CSF India ink positive, cryptococcal antigen titre 1:1024. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Perform a repeat lumbar puncture to lower intracranial pressure
Start oral fluconazole 800 mg loading dose
Start intravenous liposomal amphotericin B and oral flucytosine
questions.explanation: Induction therapy with amphotericin B and flucytosine is the standard of care for cryptococcal meningitis in HIV, especially with high fungal burden and raised ICP. Starting ART too early risks IRIS. Steroids are not indicated. While raised ICP needs management, the first urgent step is antifungal therapy.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old immunocompetent woman presents with a 2-week history of gradual onset headache, low-grade fever, and fatigue. Neurological exam normal. Lumbar puncture opening pressure 15 cm H₂O, CSF shows 15 white cells, protein 0.6 g/L, glucose 2.8 mmol/L (plasma 5.6). Cryptococcal antigen positive on latex agglutination. Fungal culture shows low burden. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Start intravenous liposomal amphotericin B and oral flucytosine
Perform a CT head with contrast
questions.explanation: For immunocompetent patients with mild disease (low fungal burden, normal ICP), oral fluconazole is effective. Amphotericin-based regimens are reserved for severe disease. Steroids are not indicated. Further imaging is unnecessary as LP is already diagnostic.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man with HIV and cryptococcal meningitis on day 5 of treatment with liposomal amphotericin B and flucytosine develops sudden headache, vomiting, and a Glasgow Coma Scale drop from 15 to 12. Pupils are unequal (right 3 mm, left 5 mm, sluggish). CT head shows no mass lesion, ventricles normal, no midline shift. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Perform an urgent lumbar puncture and drain CSF
Start intravenous acyclovir for possible herpes simplex encephalitis
Increase the dose of amphotericin B
questions.explanation: The clinical picture suggests raised intracranial pressure from cryptococcal meningitis. Therapeutic lumbar puncture to drain CSF is the first-line intervention to rapidly lower ICP. Mannitol has limited evidence and steroids may worsen outcome. Acyclovir is not indicated as the setting strongly favours cryptococcal raised ICP.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man with HIV and cryptococcal meningitis is receiving conventional amphotericin B (amphotericin B deoxycholate) 0.7 mg/kg/day and oral flucytosine. On day 5, serum creatinine rises from 80 to 200 µmol/L, potassium 2.9 mmol/L, haemoglobin drop 12 g/L. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Replace amphotericin B with oral fluconazole
Reduce the dose of amphotericin B
Switch from conventional to liposomal amphotericin B
questions.explanation: Nephrotoxicity is a known side effect of conventional amphotericin B. Switching to liposomal amphotericin significantly reduces renal toxicity. Dose reduction may not be sufficient, and stopping flucytosine is not required. Saline loading may help but changing the nephrotoxic agent is key.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with HIV (CD4 20) was treated for cryptococcal meningitis with induction therapy (liposomal amphotericin + flucytosine) for 2 weeks, then consolidation with oral fluconazole. She now starts antiretroviral therapy (ART). Two weeks later, she develops new headache, fever, and neck stiffness. CSF shows 50 white cells (predominantly lymphocytes), protein 0.8 g/L, glucose 2.5 mmol/L, and cryptococcal antigen positive but culture negative. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Perform a lumbar puncture to check opening pressure and obtain fungal culture
Restart induction therapy with amphotericin B
questions.explanation: The scenario is suspicious for immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS) versus relapse. The most important immediate step is to perform a lumbar puncture to measure opening pressure and obtain CSF for culture to differentiate. Starting steroids (for IRIS) or restarting induction therapy (for relapse) should only be done after confirming the diagnosis.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old man presents with a 1-week history of headache, fever, and neck stiffness. He has no significant medical history but appears thin. HIV test is sent. Lumbar puncture is performed. Which of the following CSF findings is a red flag for imminent neurological deterioration?
questions.explanation: An opening pressure >25 cm H2O in cryptococcal meningitis indicates raised intracranial pressure, which can lead to herniation. This is the single most important CSF finding requiring urgent intervention (e.g., therapeutic lumbar drainage). Low glucose, high protein, and pleocytosis are typical but not immediate red flags.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with known HIV (CD4 200, not on ART) is diagnosed with cryptococcal meningitis after a lumbar puncture shows a positive cryptococcal antigen. She has a mild headache but is afebrile and has no neck stiffness. Initial opening pressure is 18 cm H2O. After starting antifungal therapy, which new symptom should alert you to a developing complication?
questions.explanation: Horizontal diplopia suggests an abducens nerve palsy, a classic sign of raised intracranial pressure or hydrocephalus. This is a red flag for neurological deterioration, even in mild meningitis, and warrants urgent CT head and repeat lumbar puncture. Photophobia, blurred vision, nausea, and dizziness are less specific.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man who had a renal transplant 6 months ago presents to the Emergency Department with confusion, vomiting, and a GCS of 13. He is on tacrolimus. A lumbar puncture is performed and the opening pressure is 40 cm H2O. Cryptococcal antigen is positive. Which clinical sign on examination is most concerning and requires immediate neurosurgical referral?
questions.explanation: Papilloedema is a direct sign of significantly raised intracranial pressure. In this immunocompromised patient with extremely high opening pressure, it indicates risk of cerebral herniation and mandates urgent CSF drainage. Fever, neck stiffness, and photophobia are typical meningeal signs but not emergency thresholds.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old HIV-positive man on antiretroviral therapy (CD4 150) is being treated for cryptococcal meningitis with the induction regimen. After 2 weeks, he develops sudden right-sided weakness involving the arm and leg. What is the most likely life-threatening complication?
questions.explanation: Focal neurological deficits such as hemiparesis in cryptococcal meningitis are characteristic of a cryptococcoma, a mass lesion caused by Cryptococcus. Hydrocephalus typically presents with headache, vomiting, and gait disturbance. IRIS causes generalised inflammation, not a focal deficit. Stroke and syphilis are less common in this context.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old HIV-positive man (CD4 50) was started on amphotericin B and flucytosine for cryptococcal meningitis. After 2 weeks, he improves and is changed to oral fluconazole. Ten days later, antiretroviral therapy is initiated. One week after starting ART, he develops new headache, fever, and confusion. Lumbar puncture shows raised opening pressure (30 cm H2O), and CSF cryptococcal antigen titre is similar to previous. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The timing (1 week after starting ART), clinical deterioration, and stable cryptococcal antigen without microbiological relapse point to immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS). IRIS is a paradoxical inflammatory response as the immune system recovers, and it is a recognised complication in cryptococcal meningitis. Relapse would show rising antigen or positive culture.
